serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of maple syrup is one tablespoon (approximately 20 g), commonly used as a topping for pancakes, waffles, or oatmeal, or as a sweetener in cooking and baking.

nutrient_source.title: Maple syrup — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for maple syrup (cr_id F13030002). Values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for maple syrup (FDC ID 169661) and found consistent. HPB is the primary approved source for this Singapore-context record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Maple syrup GI of 54 is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. GI=54 places maple syrup in the low GI category (≤55). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (54 × 13.4) / 100 = 7.2, based on a 20 g serving containing approximately 13.4 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (60.5 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and promotes endothelial dysfunction; sodium content is moderate at 12 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit maple syrup to no more than 1 teaspoon (approximately 7 g) as an occasional flavouring, as its extreme sugar load (60.5 g/100 g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and vascular inflammation despite its moderate potassium (212 mg/100 g) and low sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar content of 60.5 g/100 g with zero dietary fibre causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; although GI is 54 (low category), the very high sugar density makes portion control critical
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit maple syrup to trace amounts (no more than 1 teaspoon, approximately 7 g) because its sugar content of 60.5 g/100 g — with zero fibre to blunt absorption — causes significant postprandial glycaemic excursion regardless of its low GI classification.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Concentrated free sugar (60.5 g/100 g) with zero fibre raises triglycerides, promotes hepatic de novo lipogenesis, and contributes to atherogenic dyslipidaemia per AHA dietary guidelines
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat maple syrup as a high-sugar condiment and restrict intake to occasional trace use, as its concentrated free sugar content (60.5 g/100 g) elevates serum triglycerides and promotes atherogenic dyslipidaemia in line with AHA added-sugar limits of less than 25 g/day for women and 36 g/day for men.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High free sugar (60.5 g/100 g) with zero fibre promotes systemic pro-inflammatory mediators; no anti-inflammatory factors present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid maple syrup as a regular sweetener because its concentrated sugar content (60.5 g/100 g) promotes systemic inflammation through advanced glycation end-products and pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways, with no offsetting anti-inflammatory nutrients present.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Maple syrup is a natural sweetener produced by boiling and concentrating the sap of sugar maple trees (Acer saccharum), primarily harvested in Canada and the northeastern United States. The sap is collected in early spring, then reduced by evaporation to produce a thick, amber-coloured liquid with a characteristic sweet, woody, and slightly caramel-like flavour. Maple syrup is graded by colour and flavour intensity — lighter grades (Golden, Amber) have a more delicate taste, while darker grades (Dark, Very Dark) carry a stronger, more robust maple flavour. Per 100 g, maple syrup provides approximately 260 kcal, 67 g carbohydrate (of which 60.5 g is sugar, predominantly sucrose), 0.04 g protein, and 0.06 g fat, with zero dietary fibre. It contains notable amounts of potassium (212 mg/100 g) and calcium (102 mg/100 g), and trace iron (0.11 mg/100 g). Its glycemic index is approximately 54 (low GI category), but its very high sugar density means even small servings deliver a significant glycaemic load. Clinically, maple syrup is not a safe alternative sweetener for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its concentrated free sugar content. It is commonly used as a topping for pancakes, waffles, French toast, and oatmeal, and as a flavouring agent in baking, marinades, and glazes. Maple syrup is naturally vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and contains no cholesterol.
common_user_queries: Is maple syrup safe for diabetics?
How many calories in maple syrup?
Is maple syrup better than sugar?
Can I use maple syrup if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of maple syrup?
How much sugar is in maple syrup?
What nutrients does maple syrup contain?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F13030002, confidence 0.95), cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for maple syrup. GI value of 54 sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008). All core macros and secondary nutrients are supported by named approved sources.
notes: Protein_g (0.04 g/100 g) and fat_g (0.06 g/100 g) are near-trace values confirmed by HPB and consistent with USDA data — these are not zero but are nutritionally negligible; no zero-macro justification required as values are non-zero. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — maple syrup contains no dietary fibre by composition. Cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero — maple syrup is a plant-derived product containing no cholesterol. Trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy cross-reference. Saturated_fat_g of 0.01 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169661) as HPB does not report fatty acid fractionation; this is a trace value. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g, and all vitamin fields set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation, moisture, and micronutrient vitamin data not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; USDA SR Legacy reports water_g ~32.4 g/100 g but HPB is the primary source and does not report this field — flagged for optional enrichment. Glycemic load per serving calculated at 20 g serving size (1 tablespoon): GL = (54 × 13.408) / 100 = 7.2. This record is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; therefore no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules. Maple syrup is a North American commodity food with no established local-language names in the mandatory cuisine categories.
